rawText:
When heavy rain hits a city, the water has to go somewhere — and making sure it goes to the right place is a problem that urban planners think about long before the first drop falls.

Most modern cities are built on hard surfaces like concrete and asphalt, which do not absorb water the way soil and grass do.

Without a drainage system, rainwater would simply pool on streets, flood basements, and eventually damage buildings and roads.

The most common solution is a network of underground pipes and storm drains that are designed to collect water from the surface and carry it to rivers or treatment plants.

These systems are sized based on historical rainfall data, so engineers calculate how much water is expected during a heavy storm and build pipes wide enough to handle that volume.

However, climate patterns have been shifting, and storms that were once considered rare are now happening more frequently.

When rainfall exceeds the capacity that the drainage system was built for, the excess water backs up through drains, floods low-lying streets, and overwhelms pumping stations.

To address this growing risk, many cities are now adding green infrastructure alongside traditional pipes.

Green roofs are installed on top of buildings to absorb rain before it reaches the ground, and permeable pavements are laid in parking lots so that water can seep through the surface into the soil below.

Parks and open spaces are sometimes redesigned to serve as temporary water storage areas during extreme events, holding the overflow until the main system can catch up.

These nature-based solutions are not meant to replace pipes entirely but to reduce the pressure on a system that was designed for a climate that no longer exists.

Planning for heavy rain is a slow, expensive process, yet the cost of doing nothing is almost always higher.

A single major flood can cause billions of dollars in damage, disrupt transportation for days, and put lives at risk.

Cities that invest in both engineered and natural defenses give themselves the best chance of staying dry when the next big storm arrives.

overview.title: How a City Plans for Heavy Rain

sentences.text: When it rains a lot, water needs a place to go in the city.
sentences.translation: 비가 많이 오면 도시에서 물이 갈 곳이 필요해요.

sentences.text: Cities have pipes under the ground to carry the water away.
sentences.translation: 도시에는 물을 보내는 땅속 파이프가 있어요.

sentences.text: But sometimes it rains too much and the pipes cannot hold all the water.
sentences.translation: 하지만 비가 너무 많이 오면 파이프가 물을 다 담을 수 없어요.

sentences.text: So cities are also using plants and special ground to help soak up rain.
sentences.translation: 그래서 도시들은 비를 흡수하도록 식물과 특별한 바닥도 사용하고 있어요.

sentences.text: Parks can even be used to store extra water during big storms.
sentences.translation: 공원은 큰 폭풍 때 여분의 물을 저장하는 데에도 쓸 수 있어요.

sentences.text: Preparing for rain costs money, but a big flood costs much more.
sentences.translation: 비에 대비하는 데 돈이 들지만, 큰 홍수는 훨씬 더 비용이 많이 들어요.

sentences.text: The article explains how cities manage heavy rainfall through drainage systems and newer green solutions.
sentences.translation: 이 글은 도시가 배수 시스템과 새로운 친환경 해법을 통해 폭우를 관리하는 방법을 설명해요.

sentences.text: Traditional underground pipes collect rainwater and carry it to rivers or treatment plants.
sentences.translation: 전통적인 지하 파이프는 빗물을 모아 강이나 처리 시설로 보내요.

sentences.text: Climate change is making storms more frequent, pushing these systems past their designed capacity.
sentences.translation: 기후 변화로 폭풍이 더 잦아지면서 이 시스템들이 설계 용량을 초과하고 있어요.

sentences.text: Green roofs, permeable pavements, and redesigned parks now complement traditional pipes.
sentences.translation: 녹색 지붕, 투수성 포장, 재설계된 공원이 이제 전통적인 파이프를 보완하고 있어요.

sentences.text: These nature-based methods aim to reduce strain on aging infrastructure, not replace it entirely.
sentences.translation: 이러한 자연 기반 방법은 노후 인프라의 부담을 줄이는 것이지 완전히 대체하는 것이 아니에요.

sentences.text: The writer argues that investing in both engineered and natural defenses is far cheaper than recovering from floods.
sentences.translation: 글쓴이는 공학적 방어와 자연 방어 모두에 투자하는 것이 홍수 복구보다 훨씬 저렴하다고 주장해요.

sentences.text: This expository article uses a problem-solution framework to explain urban stormwater management for an intermediate English audience.
sentences.translation: 이 설명문은 문제-해결 틀을 사용해 중급 영어 독자를 위해 도시 우수 관리를 설명해요.

sentences.text: Passive constructions like "are built," "are designed," and "are installed" recur throughout, modeling how process descriptions rely on the passive voice to foreground the object over the agent.
sentences.translation: "are built," "are designed," "are installed" 같은 수동태 구문이 반복되어, 과정 설명에서 행위자보다 대상을 부각하는 수동태 사용법을 보여줘요.

sentences.text: Sequence markers and causal connectors — "so," "however," "when," "to address" — guide readers through a multi-step explanation without losing coherence.
sentences.translation: "so," "however," "when," "to address" 같은 순서 표지와 인과 접속어가 독자를 여러 단계의 설명 속에서 일관성을 잃지 않게 안내해요.

sentences.text: The vocabulary shifts between general urban terms (city, streets, buildings) and technical infrastructure language (drainage, permeable, pumping stations), making it a useful bridge text for learners.
sentences.translation: 일반적인 도시 어휘(city, streets, buildings)와 기술적 인프라 용어(drainage, permeable, pumping stations) 사이를 오가며, 학습자에게 유용한 가교 텍스트 역할을 해요.

sentences.text: The contrast between "designed for" a past climate and "no longer exists" creates urgency without resorting to emotional language.
sentences.translation: 과거 기후에 "designed for"된 것과 "no longer exists"의 대비가 감정적 언어에 기대지 않으면서 긴급함을 만들어요.

sentences.text: The closing sentence employs a conditional structure with "when," not "if," signaling certainty that future storms will come — a subtle rhetorical move that reinforces the article's call to invest.
sentences.translation: 마지막 문장은 "if"가 아닌 "when"을 사용한 조건 구조로, 미래 폭풍이 올 것이라는 확실성을 나타내며 투자 촉구라는 글의 메시지를 강화하는 미묘한 수사적 장치예요.

sentences.original: When heavy rain hits a city, the water has to go somewhere — and making sure it goes to the right place is a problem that urban planners think about long before the first drop falls.
sentences.translation: 폭우가 도시를 강타하면 물은 어딘가로 가야 한다 — 그리고 물이 올바른 곳으로 가도록 하는 것은 첫 빗방울이 떨어지기 훨씬 전부터 도시 계획자들이 고민하는 문제이다.

grammarElements.text: When heavy rain hits a city
grammarElements.label: 시간절 (When)

grammarElements.text: the water has to go somewhere
grammarElements.label: 주절 (has to 의무)

grammarElements.text: making sure it goes to the right place
grammarElements.label: 주어 (동명사구)

grammarElements.text: a problem that urban planners think about
grammarElements.label: 보어 + 관계절

grammarElements.text: long before the first drop falls
grammarElements.label: 시간 부사절

vocabulary.example: Heavy rain caused flooding last night.
Urban areas grow quickly.
City planners design public spaces.

expressions.usage: 물리적 필연성을 간결하게 표현할 때 쓴다. has to는 의무/필연을 나타낸다
어떤 결과를 보장하기 위한 행위를 나타낼 때 쓴다

patterns.pattern: When [event] hits [place], [consequence] — and [gerund phrase] is a problem that [people] think about
patterns.explanation: 사건이 발생했을 때의 결과를 먼저 제시하고, 대시 뒤에 그 문제를 누가 다루는지 설명하는 도입 패턴.
examples: When a heatwave hits a city, the temperature soars — and keeping people safe is a challenge that officials plan for in advance.
When a virus hits a community, hospitals fill up — and preventing spread is a task that health workers prepare for year-round.

drillQuestions.question: When heavy rain _____ a city, the water has to go somewhere.
Making sure it goes to the right place is a _____ that planners think about.
"Urban planners" are people who plan for _____.
Planners think about rain _____ the first drop falls.

sentences.notes: 글의 도입부로, 대시(—)를 사용해 두 아이디어를 연결한다. 동명사 주어(making sure ~)가 도시 계획의 본질을 한 문장에 담아낸다. "long before"가 사전 계획의 중요성을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: When heavy rain hits a city
the water has to go somewhere
making sure it goes to the right place
koreanAlignment.ko: 물이 올바른 곳으로 가도록 하는 것은

koreanAlignment.en: is a problem that urban planners think about
long before the first drop falls

pronunciationNotes: urban: /ˈɜːrbən/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "어번"에 가깝다. "얼반"이 아님에 주의.
planners: /ˈplænərz/ — 첫 음절에 강세. pl을 한 번에 발음. "플래너즈"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Most modern cities are built on hard surfaces like concrete and asphalt, which do not absorb water the way soil and grass do.
sentences.translation: 대부분의 현대 도시는 콘크리트와 아스팔트 같은 단단한 표면 위에 지어지는데, 이것들은 흙과 잔디가 물을 흡수하는 방식으로 물을 흡수하지 못한다.

grammarElements.text: on hard surfaces like concrete and asphalt
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (장소)

grammarElements.text: which do not absorb water the way soil and grass do
grammarElements.label: 계속적 관계절 (which)

vocabulary.example: The sidewalk is made of concrete.
The road was paved with asphalt.
A sponge absorbs water quickly.

expressions.usage: 수동태로 도시나 건물이 특정 표면 위에 건설되었음을 나타낸다
비교 대상의 행위 방식을 참조할 때 쓰는 구문

patterns.pattern: [Subject] are built on [surface], which do not [verb] the way [natural alternative] do
patterns.explanation: 수동태로 인공 환경의 특성을 설명하고, 관계절로 자연 환경과 대비시키는 패턴.
examples: Most parking lots are covered with asphalt, which does not cool the air the way trees and shade do.
Many playgrounds are made of rubber, which does not drain water the way gravel and sand do.

drillQuestions.question: Most modern cities are _____ on hard surfaces.
Concrete and asphalt do not _____ water like soil does.
"Are built" is an example of the _____ voice.
Soil and grass _____ absorb water effectively.

sentences.notes: 첫 번째 수동태(are built) 등장. 도시가 "지어진" 환경임을 수동태로 표현하고, "the way soil and grass do"로 자연과 인공의 차이를 대비한다. 이 글에서 수동태가 반복적으로 쓰이는 시작점이다.

koreanAlignment.en: the way soil and grass do

pronunciationNotes: asphalt: /ˈæsfɔːlt/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "애스폴트"에 가깝다. ph가 /f/로 발음됨에 주의.
absorb: /əbˈzɔːrb/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "앱조브"에 가깝다. b 뒤의 s가 /z/로 발음된다.

sentences.original: Without a drainage system, rainwater would simply pool on streets, flood basements, and eventually damage buildings and roads.
sentences.translation: 배수 시스템이 없다면, 빗물은 거리에 고이고, 지하실을 침수시키며, 결국 건물과 도로를 손상시킬 것이다.

grammarElements.text: and eventually damage buildings and roads
grammarElements.label: 병렬 동사 3

vocabulary.example: The drainage system handles stormwater.
Water pooled on the flat roof.
Many basements flooded during the storm.

expressions.usage: without + 명사로 가정적 상황을 간결하게 나타낸다. would와 함께 가정법 효과를 낸다
가정 상황에서 필연적 결과를 나타낼 때 쓴다. simply가 불가피성을 강조한다

patterns.pattern: Without [system], [subject] would [result A], [result B], and eventually [result C]
patterns.explanation: without으로 가정 조건을 제시하고, 세 가지 부정적 결과를 순서대로 나열하며 심각성을 높이는 패턴.
examples: Without traffic lights, cars would collide at intersections, block emergency vehicles, and eventually cause gridlock.
Without refrigeration, food would spoil within hours, spread bacteria, and eventually cause illness.

drillQuestions.question: _____ a drainage system, rainwater would pool on streets.
Rainwater would pool, flood basements, and eventually _____ buildings.
"Would simply pool" uses "would" because it describes a _____ situation.
"Drainage" refers to a system that _____ water away.

sentences.notes: "Without ~ would ~" 구조로 배수 시스템의 필요성을 역설한다. 세 동사(pool, flood, damage)의 병렬이 점차 심각해지는 결과를 보여주며, "eventually"가 시간 순서를 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: rainwater would simply pool on streets
and eventually damage buildings and roads

pronunciationNotes: drainage: /ˈdreɪnɪdʒ/ — 첫 음절에 강세. dr을 한 번에 발음. "드레이니지"에 가깝다.
basements: /ˈbeɪsmənts/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "베이스먼츠"에 가깝다. -ments를 빠르게 발음한다.

sentences.original: The most common solution is a network of underground pipes and storm drains that are designed to collect water from the surface and carry it to rivers or treatment plants.
sentences.translation: 가장 일반적인 해결책은 지표면에서 물을 모아 강이나 처리 시설로 운반하도록 설계된 지하 파이프와 빗물 배수구의 네트워크이다.

grammarElements.text: a network of underground pipes and storm drains
grammarElements.label: 보어

grammarElements.text: that are designed to collect water from the surface and carry it to rivers or treatment plants
grammarElements.label: 관계절 (수동태 — are designed to)

vocabulary.example: The city has a network of underground tunnels.
Underground pipes carry water away from streets.
Storm drains prevent streets from flooding.
Water is cleaned at treatment plants.

expressions.usage: 수동태로 시스템이나 물건의 의도된 기능을 설명할 때 쓴다
여러 방법 중 가장 보편적인 것을 소개할 때 쓰는 표현

patterns.pattern: The most common solution is [noun phrase] that are designed to [function A] and [function B]
patterns.explanation: 가장 보편적인 해결책을 소개하고, 수동태 관계절로 그 시스템의 설계 목적을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: The most common solution is a series of filters that are designed to remove pollutants and improve water quality.
The most common solution is a set of sensors that are designed to detect motion and alert security.

drillQuestions.question: The most common solution is a _____ of underground pipes.
Storm drains are _____ to collect water from the surface.
"Are designed to" is a _____ voice construction.
The pipes carry water to rivers or _____ plants.

sentences.notes: 수동태(are designed to)가 시스템의 의도된 기능을 설명한다. "collect ~ and carry ~"의 병렬 구조가 파이프의 두 가지 역할을 명확히 한다. 과정 설명 글에서 흔한 수동태 활용이다.

koreanAlignment.en: is a network of underground pipes and storm drains
that are designed to collect water from the surface
and carry it to rivers or treatment plants

pronunciationNotes: underground: /ˌʌndərˈɡraʊnd/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "언더그라운드"에 가깝다.
treatment: /ˈtriːtmənt/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "트릿먼트"에 가깝다. tr을 한 번에 발음한다.

sentences.original: These systems are sized based on historical rainfall data, so engineers calculate how much water is expected during a heavy storm and build pipes wide enough to handle that volume.
sentences.translation: 이 시스템들은 과거 강우 데이터를 기반으로 크기가 결정되며, 따라서 엔지니어들은 폭우 시 예상되는 수량을 계산하고 그 양을 처리할 만큼 충분히 넓은 파이프를 건설한다.

grammarElements.text: engineers calculate how much water is expected
grammarElements.label: 간접의문문 (수동태 포함)

grammarElements.text: and build pipes wide enough to handle that volume
grammarElements.label: 병렬 동사구

vocabulary.example: The pipes are sized for maximum flow.
Historical data helps predict future trends.
The pipe handles a large volume of water.

expressions.usage: 수동태로 설계 기준을 설명할 때 쓴다. based on이 근거를 나타낸다
"형용사 + enough to + 동사"로 충분한 정도를 표현한다

patterns.pattern: [System] are sized based on [data], so [people] calculate [measure] and build [structure] [adjective] enough to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 수동태로 시스템 설계 기준을 설명하고, so로 그에 따른 구체적 과정을 이어가는 패턴.
examples: Bridges are sized based on expected traffic data, so engineers calculate peak load and build supports strong enough to carry that weight.
Reservoirs are sized based on historical drought data, so planners calculate water demand and build storage large enough to meet that need.

drillQuestions.question: These systems are _____ based on historical rainfall data.
Engineers calculate how much water is _____ during a storm.
"Wide enough to handle" means the pipes are _____ large.
"Are sized" and "is expected" are both in the _____ voice.

sentences.notes: 수동태가 두 번(are sized, is expected) 등장해 설계 과정의 객관성을 강조한다. "enough to + 동사" 구조가 설계 목표를 명확히 한다. so가 설계 기준과 실행을 인과적으로 연결한다.

koreanAlignment.en: how much water is expected during a heavy storm
and build pipes wide enough to handle that volume
koreanAlignment.ko: 그 양을 처리할 만큼 충분히 넓은 파이프를 건설한다

pronunciationNotes: historical: /hɪˈstɔːrɪkəl/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "히스토리컬"에 가깝다. h를 가볍게 발음한다.
volume: /ˈvɑːljuːm/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "볼륨"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: However, climate patterns have been shifting, and storms that were once considered rare are now happening more frequently.
sentences.translation: 하지만, 기후 패턴이 변화해 왔고, 한때 드물다고 여겨졌던 폭풍이 이제 더 자주 발생하고 있다.

grammarElements.text: storms that were once considered rare
grammarElements.label: 주어 + 관계절 (수동태)

vocabulary.example: Climate change affects weather patterns.
Weather patterns have been shifting over decades.
The event was once considered unlikely.
Storms are happening more frequently.

expressions.usage: 현재완료진행형으로 과거부터 현재까지 지속되는 변화를 나타낸다
수동태로 과거의 인식을 전달하며 현재와의 대조를 만든다

patterns.pattern: However, [subject] have been [verb-ing], and [things] that were once considered [adjective] are now [verb-ing] more [adverb]
patterns.explanation: However로 논점을 전환하고, 과거와 현재의 변화를 대비시키는 패턴. 기후나 사회 변화를 설명할 때 효과적이다.
examples: However, sea levels have been rising, and events that were once considered extreme are now occurring more regularly.
However, energy prices have been climbing, and costs that were once considered manageable are now becoming more burdensome.

drillQuestions.question: Climate patterns have been _____ over the years.
Storms that were once considered _____ are now more common.
"Were once considered" is in the _____ voice.
"However" signals a _____ from the previous point.

sentences.notes: However로 전환점을 만드는 핵심 문장. 현재완료진행(have been shifting)과 수동태 관계절(were once considered rare)이 변화의 지속성을 강조한다. 과거와 현재의 대비가 문제의 긴급성을 부각한다.

koreanAlignment.en: and storms that were once considered rare

pronunciationNotes: climate: /ˈklaɪmət/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "클라이밋"에 가깝다. 두 번째 음절은 약하게.
frequently: /ˈfriːkwəntli/ — 첫 음절에 강세. qu를 /kw/로 한 번에 발음. "프리퀀틀리"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: When rainfall exceeds the capacity that the drainage system was built for, the excess water backs up through drains, floods low-lying streets, and overwhelms pumping stations.
sentences.translation: 강우량이 배수 시스템이 감당하도록 만들어진 용량을 초과하면, 초과된 물은 배수구를 역류하고, 저지대 거리를 침수시키며, 펌프장을 마비시킨다.

grammarElements.text: that the drainage system was built for
grammarElements.label: 관계절 (수동태 — was built for)

vocabulary.example: Demand exceeds supply during peak hours.
The stadium is filled to capacity.
Excess water flows into the overflow tank.
Too much rain overwhelms the drains.

expressions.usage: 물이나 하수가 원래 방향의 반대로 올라올 때 쓰는 표현
수동태로 시스템의 원래 설계 목적을 나타낸다

patterns.pattern: When [measure] exceeds the capacity that [system] was built for, [result A], [result B], and [result C]
patterns.explanation: 시스템의 한계를 초과했을 때의 연쇄 결과를 세 가지로 나열하는 패턴.
examples: When traffic exceeds the capacity that the road was built for, delays increase, accidents multiply, and emergency access becomes impossible.
When enrollment exceeds the capacity that the school was built for, classrooms overflow, teachers struggle, and quality drops.

drillQuestions.question: When rainfall _____ the capacity, flooding occurs.
The drainage system was _____ for a certain amount of rain.
Excess water backs up, floods streets, and _____ pumping stations.
"Low-lying streets" are streets that are _____ in elevation.

sentences.notes: 수동태(was built for)가 과거 설계 기준을 나타내고, 세 동사(backs up, floods, overwhelms)의 병렬이 시스템 실패의 연쇄를 생생하게 보여준다. "exceeds the capacity"가 핵심 문제를 정의한다.

koreanAlignment.en: that the drainage system was built for
the excess water backs up through drains

pronunciationNotes: exceeds: /ɪkˈsiːdz/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "익시즈"에 가깝다. x가 /ks/로 발음된다.
overwhelms: /ˌoʊvərˈwelmz/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "오버웰름즈"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: To address this growing risk, many cities are now adding green infrastructure alongside traditional pipes.
sentences.translation: 이 증가하는 위험에 대응하기 위해, 많은 도시들이 현재 전통적인 파이프와 함께 녹색 인프라를 추가하고 있다.

vocabulary.example: The government must address the housing problem.
The city invested heavily in infrastructure.
New methods work alongside traditional ones.

expressions.usage: to 부정사로 행동의 목적을 문두에 제시할 때 쓴다. address는 "문제에 대처하다"라는 뜻
자연 기반의 도시 기반 시설을 가리키는 전문 용어

patterns.pattern: To address [problem], many [organizations] are now [verb-ing] [new solution] alongside [existing solution]
patterns.explanation: 문두에 목적을 밝히고, 기존 방법과 새로운 방법을 병행하는 해결책을 소개하는 패턴.
examples: To address rising costs, many hospitals are now using telemedicine alongside in-person visits.
To address traffic congestion, many cities are now building bike lanes alongside car roads.

drillQuestions.question: _____ address this growing risk, cities are adding green infrastructure.
Green infrastructure is added _____ traditional pipes.
"Are now adding" uses the present _____ tense.
"Infrastructure" means the basic _____ and systems of a place.

sentences.notes: 전환 문장으로, 문제에서 해결책으로 넘어간다. "To address ~"가 문두에서 목적을 명확히 하고, "alongside"가 기존 방법과의 보완 관계를 강조한다. "green infrastructure"가 핵심 개념을 도입한다.
pronunciationNotes: infrastructure: /ˈɪnfrəstrʌktʃər/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "인프라스트럭처"에 가깝다. 음절이 많으므로 끊어 읽는 연습이 필요하다.
alongside: /əˌlɔːŋˈsaɪd/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "얼롱사이드"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Green roofs are installed on top of buildings to absorb rain before it reaches the ground, and permeable pavements are laid in parking lots so that water can seep through the surface into the soil below.
sentences.translation: 녹색 지붕은 빗물이 지면에 도달하기 전에 흡수하도록 건물 위에 설치되고, 투수성 포장재는 물이 표면을 통해 아래 토양으로 스며들 수 있도록 주차장에 깔린다.

grammarElements.text: to absorb rain before it reaches the ground
grammarElements.label: to 부정사구 (목적) + 시간절

grammarElements.text: in parking lots so that water can seep through the surface into the soil below
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 + so that 목적절

vocabulary.example: Solar panels are installed on rooftops.
Permeable surfaces let water pass through.
The city replaced old pavements with permeable ones.
Water seeps through cracks in the wall.

expressions.usage: 수동태로 구조물의 설치 위치를 설명할 때 쓴다
행위의 목적이나 의도한 결과를 설명할 때 쓰는 접속사

patterns.pattern: [Solution A] are installed [location] to [purpose], and [Solution B] are laid [location] so that [result]
patterns.explanation: 두 가지 수동태 구문을 병렬로 연결해 각각의 녹색 인프라 설치와 목적을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: Solar panels are installed on rooftops to generate electricity, and charging stations are placed in garages so that residents can power their cars.
Speed bumps are installed near schools to slow traffic, and crosswalks are painted at every corner so that children can cross safely.

drillQuestions.question: Green roofs are _____ on top of buildings.
Permeable pavements are _____ in parking lots.
"So that water can seep" explains the _____ of the pavement.
"Permeable" means water can _____ through the surface.

sentences.notes: 수동태가 두 번(are installed, are laid) 등장해 설치 과정을 설명한다. "to absorb"와 "so that ~ can seep"이 각 시설의 목적을 명확히 한다. 기술적 어휘(permeable, seep)가 집중적으로 등장하는 문장이다.

koreanAlignment.en: Green roofs are installed on top of buildings
to absorb rain before it reaches the ground
and permeable pavements are laid in parking lots
so that water can seep through the surface into the soil below
koreanAlignment.ko: 물이 표면을 통해 아래 토양으로 스며들 수 있도록

pronunciationNotes: permeable: /ˈpɜːrmiəbl/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "퍼미어블"에 가깝다. -eable을 빠르게 발음한다.
seep: /siːp/ — 장모음 /iː/. "씹"에 가깝다. 짧은 단어지만 길게 발음해야 한다.

sentences.original: Parks and open spaces are sometimes redesigned to serve as temporary water storage areas during extreme events, holding the overflow until the main system can catch up.
sentences.translation: 공원과 열린 공간은 극한 상황 시 임시 저수 구역 역할을 하도록 재설계되기도 하며, 주 시스템이 따라잡을 때까지 넘치는 물을 담아 둔다.

grammarElements.text: to serve as temporary water storage areas
grammarElements.label: to 부정사구 (목적)

grammarElements.text: holding the overflow until the main system can catch up
grammarElements.label: 분사구문 (부대상황)

vocabulary.example: The park was redesigned to include a rain garden.
The shelter is only a temporary solution.
The overflow flooded nearby streets.

expressions.usage: 어떤 것이 원래 목적 외의 기능을 수행할 때 쓴다
뒤처진 것이 정상 속도나 수준으로 돌아올 때 쓰는 구동사

patterns.pattern: [Spaces] are sometimes redesigned to serve as [function] during [event], [participle phrase]
patterns.explanation: 수동태로 공간의 재설계를 설명하고, 분사구문으로 부가적 기능을 덧붙이는 패턴.
examples: Stadiums are sometimes redesigned to serve as emergency shelters during natural disasters, housing thousands until conditions improve.
School gyms are sometimes redesigned to serve as vaccination centers during outbreaks, processing residents until the demand subsides.

drillQuestions.question: Parks are sometimes _____ to serve as water storage areas.
They hold the overflow _____ the main system can catch up.
"Serve as" means to function _____ something.
"Holding the overflow" is a _____ phrase that adds extra information.

sentences.notes: 수동태(are redesigned)가 공간의 변환을 설명한다. 분사구문(holding the overflow ~)이 부가적 기능을 간결하게 전달한다. "until ~ can catch up"이 시간 제한을 나타내 임시성을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: are sometimes redesigned to serve as
holding the overflow until the main system can catch up
koreanAlignment.ko: 주 시스템이 따라잡을 때까지 넘치는 물을 담아 둔다

pronunciationNotes: temporary: /ˈtempəreri/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "템퍼레리"에 가깝다. 네 음절 모두 빠르게 이어 발음한다.
overflow: /ˈoʊvərfloʊ/ — 명사일 때 첫 음절에 강세. "오버플로우"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: These nature-based solutions are not meant to replace pipes entirely but to reduce the pressure on a system that was designed for a climate that no longer exists.
sentences.translation: 이러한 자연 기반 해결책은 파이프를 완전히 대체하려는 것이 아니라, 더 이상 존재하지 않는 기후를 위해 설계된 시스템에 대한 부담을 줄이기 위한 것이다.

grammarElements.text: are not meant to replace pipes entirely
grammarElements.label: 동사 (수동태 — are not meant to)

grammarElements.text: on a system that was designed for a climate
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 + 관계절 (수동태)

vocabulary.example: Nature-based solutions use natural processes.
This tool is meant to help, not harm.
The system is under too much pressure.
The plan was not entirely successful.

expressions.phrase: are not meant to ... but to
expressions.usage: 수동태로 의도를 부정한 뒤 실제 목적을 제시하는 대조 구문
과거에는 있었지만 현재는 사라진 상태를 표현할 때 쓴다

patterns.pattern: [Solutions] are not meant to [A] entirely but to [B] on a system that was designed for [context] that no longer exists
patterns.explanation: 수동태로 해결책의 범위를 한정하고, 이중 관계절로 기존 시스템의 한계를 설명하는 패턴.
examples: These reforms are not meant to replace the law entirely but to update the rules within a framework that was designed for an economy that no longer exists.
These patches are not meant to fix the software entirely but to ease the burden on a codebase that was written for hardware that no longer exists.

drillQuestions.question: These solutions are not _____ to replace pipes entirely.
The system was _____ for a different climate.
"No longer exists" means it _____ exist anymore.
The goal is to reduce the _____ on the old system.

sentences.notes: "not meant to ~ but to ~" 구조가 해결책의 범위를 명확히 한다. 이중 관계절(that was designed for ~ that no longer exists)이 핵심 메시지를 담는다. 수동태(are not meant, was designed)가 객관적 서술 톤을 유지한다.

koreanAlignment.en: are not meant to replace pipes entirely
on a system that was designed for a climate

pronunciationNotes: entirely: /ɪnˈtaɪərli/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "인타이얼리"에 가깝다.
pressure: /ˈpreʃər/ — 첫 음절에 강세. ss가 /ʃ/로 발음됨. "프레셔"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Planning for heavy rain is a slow, expensive process, yet the cost of doing nothing is almost always higher.
sentences.translation: 폭우에 대한 계획은 느리고 비용이 많이 드는 과정이지만, 아무것도 하지 않는 비용은 거의 항상 더 높다.

vocabulary.example: Building new infrastructure is a long process.
Flood prevention is expensive but necessary.
The cost of inaction can be enormous.

expressions.usage: 무행동의 대가가 더 크다는 것을 강조할 때 쓰는 표현
예외가 거의 없는 규칙성을 나타낼 때 쓴다

patterns.pattern: [Gerund phrase] is a [adjective], [adjective] process, yet the cost of doing nothing is [comparative]
patterns.explanation: 동명사 주어로 행동의 어려움을 인정한 뒤, yet으로 전환해 무행동의 더 큰 비용을 강조하는 패턴.
examples: Upgrading the power grid is a complex, costly process, yet the cost of doing nothing is almost always greater.
Retraining employees is a slow, demanding process, yet the cost of doing nothing is almost always steeper.

drillQuestions.question: Planning for heavy rain is a slow, _____ process.
The cost of doing _____ is almost always higher.
"Yet" is similar in meaning to _____.
"Almost always higher" means _____ higher in most cases.

sentences.notes: 양보 구조(~ yet ~)로 투자의 필요성을 역설한다. "the cost of doing nothing"이라는 표현이 무행동의 대가를 구체화한다. 동명사 주어 두 개(Planning ~, the cost of doing nothing)가 대칭을 이룬다.

koreanAlignment.en: yet the cost of doing nothing

pronunciationNotes: expensive: /ɪkˈspensɪv/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "익스펜시브"에 가깝다.
process: /ˈprɑːses/ — 미국식은 첫 음절에 강세, /ˈprɑːses/. "프라세스"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: A single major flood can cause billions of dollars in damage, disrupt transportation for days, and put lives at risk.
sentences.translation: 단 한 번의 대규모 홍수가 수십억 달러의 피해를 초래하고, 며칠간 교통을 마비시키며, 생명을 위험에 빠뜨릴 수 있다.

vocabulary.example: The flood destroyed hundreds of homes.
The disaster cost billions of dollars.
The storm disrupted train services.

expressions.usage: 재해의 경제적 규모를 구체적으로 나타낼 때 쓴다
인명 피해의 가능성을 경고할 때 쓰는 관용 표현

patterns.pattern: A single [event] can [economic impact], [operational impact], and [human impact]
patterns.explanation: 단 하나의 사건이 경제적, 운영적, 인명 피해를 동시에 초래함을 세 가지로 나열하는 패턴. 심각성을 높이는 순서로 배열한다.
examples: A single earthquake can destroy entire neighborhoods, shut down hospitals for weeks, and claim thousands of lives.
A single cyberattack can cost millions in lost revenue, cripple essential services, and expose private data.

drillQuestions.question: A single major flood can _____ billions of dollars in damage.
A flood can disrupt transportation for _____.
"Put lives at risk" means to _____ people in danger.
"A single major flood" emphasizes that _____ one flood can be devastating.

sentences.notes: 세 동사(cause, disrupt, put)의 병렬이 홍수 피해의 다층적 심각성을 보여준다. "A single"이 단 한 번의 사건으로도 충분하다는 경각심을 주고, 피해가 경제 → 교통 → 인명 순으로 심각해진다.

koreanAlignment.en: can cause billions of dollars in damage

pronunciationNotes: disrupt: /dɪsˈrʌpt/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "디스럽트"에 가깝다. 끝의 -pt를 또렷이 발음한다.
billions: /ˈbɪljənz/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "빌리언즈"에 가깝다. l 뒤의 y 연결에 주의.

sentences.original: Cities that invest in both engineered and natural defenses give themselves the best chance of staying dry when the next big storm arrives.
sentences.translation: 공학적 방어와 자연 방어 모두에 투자하는 도시는 다음 큰 폭풍이 올 때 건조하게 유지될 최고의 기회를 스스로에게 부여한다.

grammarElements.text: Cities that invest in both engineered and natural defenses
grammarElements.label: 주어 + 관계절

grammarElements.text: the best chance of staying dry
grammarElements.label: 직접목적어 + 동명사구

grammarElements.text: when the next big storm arrives
grammarElements.label: 시간절 (when)

vocabulary.example: Cities should invest in flood prevention.
Engineered solutions include pipes and pumps.
Flood defenses protect the coastal city.

expressions.usage: 적극적 행동으로 최선의 결과를 만들어 낸다는 뜻. 재귀대명사가 주체의 자율성을 강조한다

expressions.phrase: when the next big storm arrives
expressions.usage: "if"가 아닌 "when"을 써서 폭풍이 올 것임을 확실시하는 표현

patterns.pattern: [Subject] that invest in both [A] and [B] give themselves the best chance of [gerund] when [future event]
patterns.explanation: 두 가지에 모두 투자하는 주체가 미래 사건에 가장 잘 대비한다는 결론을 내리는 패턴. 글의 마무리에 적합하다.
examples: Students that invest in both study habits and mental health give themselves the best chance of succeeding when exams arrive.
Companies that invest in both technology and training give themselves the best chance of adapting when markets shift.

drillQuestions.question: Cities that _____ in both types of defenses are best prepared.
They give _____ the best chance of staying dry.
"When the next big storm arrives" uses "when" instead of "if" to show _____.
"Engineered and natural defenses" refers to _____ types of flood protection.

sentences.notes: 글의 결론이자 행동 촉구 문장. "both ~ and ~"가 이중 전략의 중요성을 강조하고, "when"(if가 아님)이 미래 폭풍의 확실성을 나타낸다. 재귀대명사(themselves)가 도시의 자율적 행동을 부각한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Cities that invest in both engineered and natural defenses
koreanAlignment.ko: 공학적 방어와 자연 방어 모두에 투자하는 도시는

koreanAlignment.en: the best chance of staying dry
when the next big storm arrives

pronunciationNotes: engineered: /ˌendʒɪˈnɪrd/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "엔지니어드"에 가깝다. -ed가 /d/로 발음된다.
defenses: /dɪˈfensɪz/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "디펜시즈"에 가깝다. 복수형 -es를 /ɪz/로 발음한다.
